title: Fitting perimeter insulation to cut the floor-edge cold bridge
subtitle: Insulating the slab edge where the ground floor meets the external wall
excerpt: The junction between a ground floor concrete slab and the external wall is the most common linear cold bridge in UK dwellings. Adding 50 mm of EPS or PIR board around the slab perimeter, inside the skirting line, reduces the linear thermal transmittance of this junction from around 0.15 W/mK down to 0.05 W/mK. The work is most practical when the floor is already being renovated for a new screed or underfloor heating.
sourceNotes: BRE IP 1/06 for linear thermal transmittance of floor-wall junctions. gov.uk Part L1B (2022) for floor U-value targets in existing dwellings. CIBSE TM54 for thermal bridging corrections in whole-building energy calculations.

glossaryTerms.definition: Heat loss through elements that conduct faster than the surrounding insulation. At the floor perimeter, the concrete slab edge connects the warm interior to the cold external wall, creating a linear cold bridge around the entire room perimeter.

The heat loss per metre of a linear junction, such as a floor-wall meeting point, in W/mK. A lower psi-value means less heat loss per metre of that junction. Floor-edge bridges typically range from 0.06 to 0.20 W/mK depending on the construction detail.

A sand-cement or calcium-sulphate finish layer laid over a concrete slab to create a level surface for floor finishes. When relaying screed, perimeter insulation is most easily fitted before the screed is poured.

Expanded polystyrene rigid insulation board. Cheaper and easier to cut than PIR. Thermal conductivity around 0.033 W/mK, requiring slightly more depth than PIR for the same U-value. Suitable for perimeter insulation where the wall will cover the edge.

content.text: In a solid ground floor, the concrete slab runs from wall to wall and its exposed edge sits against the external wall cavity or inner leaf. Heat travels through the slab edge and into the cold wall at a rate characterised by its
linear thermal transmittance

. In a 6 m x 5 m room the total perimeter is 22 m. At a psi-value of 0.15 W/mK and a 10 K indoor-outdoor temperature difference, that junction alone loses 33 watts continuously in winter. Fitting 50 mm of
EPS board
around the slab perimeter inside the skirting cuts that figure by around 60 percent.

attrs.title: Best time to do this work

content.text: Perimeter insulation is most cost-effective when the floor is already being renovated. If a new screed is being poured for underfloor heating, the perimeter insulation strip goes in before the screed, costs almost nothing extra, and is invisible in the finished room. Retrofitting it after the fact means lifting the floor finish around the perimeter, which adds significant cost.
What you need
EPS or PIR board strip, 50 mm thick, cut to the room height from slab top to finished floor level. Total linear metres equal to the room perimeter plus 10 percent for corners.
Mechanical fixings: 80 mm hammer fixings or adhesive mortar. Make sure any mortar is rated for bonding EPS to concrete or masonry.
Compressible perimeter strip (needed when fitting before a screed): 10 mm mineral wool or foam strip, 150 mm high. This lets the screed expand at the wall junction rather than cracking.

Hammer fixings into a solid ground floor slab generate concrete dust. Wear an FFP2 mask and eye protection. Check for any underfloor services (electrical conduit, water pipes) before drilling by using a pipe and cable detector along the wall-floor junction.
Preparation

Remove the skirting boards and set aside. The insulation strip will fit between the existing wall surface and a new shorter skirting, or the original skirting can be refixed over the top if the insulation strip is no thicker than the skirting depth.
Measure the distance from the finished floor surface up to the skirting return. The insulation strip must sit flush with the finished floor surface so the skirting can cover the top edge.
Cut the EPS or PIR strip into lengths of 400 to 600 mm. Cuts at internal and external corners require mitring to 45 degrees to maintain the thermal continuity around the corner.
Fitting the insulation
Bond the EPS strip to the wall face using adhesive mortar. Press firmly and hold for 30 seconds. For exposed
screed
applications (before a new pour), use the compressible strip in place of rigid board: the compressible strip accommodates the screed expansion rather than cracking.
At internal corners, cut both boards at 45 degrees and butt the mitred faces together so there is no gap at the corner. A gap at the corner creates a point
thermal bridge
that can cause a cold spot at floor level.
Refit the skirting boards over the insulation strip. A 25 mm overhang at the floor level hides the bottom edge of the insulation and any gap between strip and floor finish.
What done well looks like

The insulation strip runs continuously around all four walls without gaps at corners. Skirting boards are refitted and the room looks unchanged. The floor-wall junction no longer appears as a cold stripe on a thermal camera scan in winter. Underfloor heating (if fitted) reaches temperature more quickly because the slab perimeter is no longer a cold heat sink.
What done badly looks like

The insulation strip was fitted on three walls but not the fourth (the external wall only). The cold bridge at the internal party wall junction was ignored. Corner gaps were filled with expanding foam instead of mitred board, leaving a compressible foam cold bridge. The skirting was refitted without any insulation behind it, clamping a warm wooden board over an uninsulated slab edge and providing no reduction in psi-value.
Troubleshooter

attrs.symptom: Screed has cracked at the wall junction after pouring
attrs.cause: Rigid EPS board was used instead of compressible strip against the wall before screed, preventing thermal expansion
attrs.fix: For future rooms, use a compressible perimeter strip rated for screed applications. For the cracked room, rout out the crack, fill with flexible polyurethane sealant, and allow to cure before laying floor finish.

attrs.symptom: Cold stripe still visible at floor level on thermal camera despite insulation
attrs.cause: Insulation strip does not extend below the screed surface to the underside of the slab
attrs.fix: The strip must reach the underside of the structural slab, not just the screed surface. If only screed depth was covered, the slab edge below the screed is still a cold bridge of equal magnitude.

attrs.symptom: Skirting has bowed outward away from the wall after refitting over insulation
attrs.cause: Insulation strip is thicker than the skirting depth, forcing it to lean away from the wall
attrs.fix: Use 25 mm insulation strip rather than 50 mm if skirting depth is limited. The 25 mm strip still reduces the psi-value by around 40 percent and can be covered by standard 19 mm skirting.
